claimed_contribution: The paper explicitly demonstrates a potential upward bias in logit-estimated promotion-induced brand-switching elasticities when consumers are forward looking.
It develops a dynamic structural model of joint purchase incidence and brand choice under promotion uncertainty, incorporating inventory, stockout, state dependence, and continuous heterogeneity.
It shows, through simulation, empirical estimation, and policy simulation, that reduced-form incidence models can only partially mitigate this bias.
context: The empirical setting is the U.S. ketchup category with weekly scanner panel data and frequent price promotions across major Heinz, Hunt's, and Del Monte brand-size alternatives. The calibration data are from Sioux Falls, South Dakota, and holdout validation data are from Springfield, Missouri.
design_and_data: The authors run three analyses: a synthetic-data simulation, model estimation on real panel data, and a promotion-policy counterfactual. The synthetic simulation generates 50 weeks of decisions for 200 consumers from the dynamic structural model and compares five alternatives (stand-alone logit, nested logit, logit with no-purchase alternative, static structural, dynamic structural). The empirical application uses ACNielsen household data with 8823 observations from 173 households over 51 weeks (1473 purchases), plus a holdout sample of 4131 observations from 81 households over 51 weeks. The structural model is a dynamic programming model with first-order Markov promotion expectations and is estimated via simulated maximum likelihood with interpolation for the value function.
main_findings: In synthetic data where the structural model is true, reduced-form models overestimate promotion-induced switching elasticities, with the stand-alone logit model most upward biased and nested logit only partially correcting the bias.
In real ketchup data, the dynamic structural model fits better than reduced-form alternatives and produces lower switching elasticities, with the pattern logit > nested logit > structural.
When promotion frequency is increased by 50% for Heinz 28/32 oz, reduced-form models predict much larger sales gains than the dynamic structural model, indicating overprediction under policy change.
one_line_summary: Using simulation and ketchup scanner panel data, the paper shows that reduced-form logit promotion models overstate brand-switching effects when consumers are forward looking and that a dynamic structural model gives better fit and more conservative elasticity and policy predictions.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior logit-based promotion-response research by integrating forward-looking purchase timing, promotion expectations, and inventory dynamics into a structural incidence-choice framework to reassess switching elasticities.
research_gap: Prior work separately examined promotion-driven brand switching with reduced-form logit models and dynamic rational purchase timing with structural models. The paper identifies a specific unresolved issue: whether ignoring forward-looking timing adjustments causes upward bias in measured promotion-induced brand switching and in policy-change forecasts.
research_question: Do conventional reduced-form logit models overestimate promotion-induced brand-switching elasticities because they do not model forward-looking consumers' purchase-timing adjustments? Can a dynamic structural model of incidence and brand choice under promotion uncertainty recover less biased switching effects and produce more credible predictions under promotion policy changes?
